scenario: Beacon Bank extracts a fixed window of 3 characters beginning at position 2 from a customer reference for 7400 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a service task input mapping must apply the three-argument substring on the literal value foobar and pass the result to the downstream worker.
question: What is the result of the FEEL three-argument substring call on the value foobar with start position 2 and length 3?

options.text: The expression returns the value bar because the start position is 0-based and the length counts characters beginning at the start index of the string
The expression returns the value oob because the start position is 1-based and the length counts the characters to include starting at that position
The expression returns the value oobar because the length argument is ignored when it is smaller than the remaining characters from the start position
The expression returns the value foo because the length is applied first to truncate the input before the start position is read from the trimmed value

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL substring positions are 1-based, so starting at 2 selects the second character o rather than the third character b as in a 0-based scheme.

b.text: Correct. The three-argument substring returns length characters from the 1-based start position, so foobar at position 2 with length 3 yields oob in line with the documented behaviour.

c.text: Incorrect. The length argument is always honoured when given; it bounds the number of characters returned from the start position.

d.text: Incorrect. The function reads the start position first and then returns up to length characters; it does not truncate the input ahead of applying the start position.

explanation: The three-argument FEEL substring returns up to length characters from the 1-based start position; foobar at start 2 length 3 yields oob, mirroring the documented substring semantics.
